text.title: Survey Design Rules and Antipatterns
text.summary: Concrete question-writing rules, common mistake table, and AI-agent gotchas for survey design.

rule.statement: Randomize answer-option order on every multiple-choice and matrix item (except scales, where order is meaningful).
rule.rationale: First-option bias inflates selection of the top-listed answer by 5-15% in self-administered surveys.

rule.statement: For pricing, never ask "would you pay X." Use Van Westendorp (4 price-sensitivity questions) plus a real price anchor or a pre-order page.
rule.rationale: Stated willingness-to-pay is 2-5x higher than actual purchase behavior. Behavioral anchors (price anchoring, actual payment) produce calibrated estimates.

rule.statement: Anchor sample size to the decision, not vanity. For a directional preference split at 95% CI with ±10pp precision, n=100 per segment is sufficient.
rule.rationale: Collecting 1,000 responses when 100 answer the question wastes fielding budget and adds no precision to the key decision metric.

rule.statement: Segment in the analysis plan before fielding. Do not slice by unplanned variables post-hoc without flagging the analysis as exploratory.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc segmentation inflates false-positive rates by increasing the number of comparisons tested on the same data.

rule.statement: Version-control the survey JSON alongside the analysis notebook. Surveys are code.
rule.rationale: Undocumented survey changes between waves make longitudinal comparisons invalid and findings unreproducible.

antipattern.description: Leading questions that presuppose an answer ("Don't you think X is better?")
antipattern.reason: Biases responses toward the implied preferred answer; data cannot be used for unbiased inference.

antipattern.description: Surveys with no screener questions at the front
antipattern.reason: Off-target respondents contaminate the sample; results apply to an undefined population.

antipattern.description: Only closed questions with no open-ended field
antipattern.reason: Misses unknown motivations and verbatim language that reveals how respondents actually think about the problem.
Confidence intervals are too wide for actionable decision-making; switch to qualitative interviews instead.

antipattern.description: No pilot test before full distribution
antipattern.reason: Confusing questions, broken logic, and unexpected completion times only surface in the pilot; fixing post-field is impossible.

section.title: AI-Agent Gotchas

rule.statement: Run bias-linter.py on every agent-drafted question set before sending to human review. Agents drift toward double-barrel items ("How easy and intuitive is...") and leading framings that they then rationalize away.
rule.rationale: Regex-based deterministic checks catch patterns the LLM rationalizes as acceptable when reviewing its own output.

section.reference: Python pre-filter detecting leading, hypothetical, absolutist, and double-barrel questions

antipattern.description: Agents use Likert-5 for everything without justifying scale choice per question
antipattern.reason: Scale choice affects response distribution; 5-point scales compress nuance for attitude items where 7-point is standard.

antipattern.description: Agent-generated demographics duplicate or omit segments tracked in user-personas.md
antipattern.reason: Mismatched demographic options prevent cross-study comparison; always pass the personas doc as context and require options to be a subset.

antipattern.description: Agent summarizes open-ended fields without disclosing how many responses were actually read
antipattern.reason: Silent corpus truncation produces hallucinated synthesis that looks like full-corpus analysis.
